title: Why 'Diamond Dogs'
moment: Casting around for a name for their new circle, Nate lands on 'the Diamond Dogs' -- a nod to Richmond's Greyhound mascot -- and Ted blesses it on the spot.
idea: Inside the story the name has nothing to do with Bowie: it's a goofy locker-room in-joke built on the club mascot, which grounds a lofty 'men's support group' idea in something silly and real.
why: A nice pairing with the Bowie cue -- the show gives an earnest in-universe reason while the credits wink at the album. Watch how fast the sincere moment turns into a bit.
source: Ted Lasso Fandom 'Diamond Dogs' entry and the scene transcript (lassoism): Nate names them for the Greyhound mascot; Ted approves.
groundingNotes: Independently re-verified via WebSearch (2026-06-19): Nate proposing 'Diamond Dogs' for Richmond's Greyhound mascot and Ted's approval corroborated across the Ted Lasso Fandom 'Diamond Dogs' page, the lassoism S01E08 scene image/transcript, and the Wikipedia E8 entry. WebFetch firewalled this session; grounding capped at 4 (no primary page openable).

title: The Bowie needle-drop
moment: The men christen their new support circle the 'Diamond Dogs' -- and David Bowie's 1974 song of the same name plays over the closing credits, a glam anthem about a ragged gang of outsiders.
idea: The title doubles as a nod to Bowie's Diamond Dogs, his concept record about a glamorous band of misfits -- which rhymes with this ragtag brotherhood of men learning to lean on each other.
why: Listen to the end credits: the Bowie track is a confirmed cue. The deeper 'did the writers name the episode after the album?' is an unconfirmed inference, so enjoy the resonance without treating it as settled intent.
source: Bowie's Diamond Dogs (1974), confirmed as this episode's end-credits song via IMDb's soundtrack listing and the Ted Lasso Fandom song page.
groundingNotes: Independently re-verified via WebSearch (2026-06-19): Bowie's 'Diamond Dogs' as the E8 end-credits song corroborated across the Ted Lasso Fandom 'Diamond Dogs (song)' page, IMDb soundtrack listing, and Tunefind. Bowie's 1974 album confirmed as a glam-rock concept record about a band of outsiders (multiple Bowie sources). Deliberate title intent remains unconfirmed -- the in-show name comes from the Greyhound mascot -- so kept at partial. WebFetch firewalled this session.

title: Roy and Keeley begin
moment: Before they're even officially together, Keeley tells Roy the truth -- that she slept with Jamie again. Roy is hurt, goes away to actually sit with it, and comes back ready to ask her to dinner.
idea: The relationship launches on candor instead of a grand gesture: vulnerability up front, time taken to process, and a choice to go slow because casual flings leave Roy feeling worse.
why: A quiet counter-model to TV romance. Watch Roy choose to feel his feelings rather than perform them -- the foundation the whole pairing is built on.
source: Wikipedia episode entry and recaps (ShowSnob, TV Tropes) of the Roy/Keeley honesty arc.
groundingNotes: Independently re-verified via WebSearch (2026-06-19): Keeley honestly disclosing she slept with Jamie again, Roy taking time to process and wanting to go slow because one-night stands leave him feeling worse, then returning to ask her to dinner -- all corroborated across two independent recaps (ShowSnob E8 recap and the TV Tropes E8 recap) plus the Wikipedia E8 entry.
